concepts.concept_title: Acid-base indicators and their colour changes
concepts.description: Indicators like litmus, turmeric, methyl orange, and phenolphthalein change colour in acidic or basic solutions. Natural indicators (litmus, turmeric) and synthetic indicators (methyl orange, phenolphthalein) can be used to distinguish between acids and bases through characteristic colour changes.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of acids based on H+ ion production
concepts.description: Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H+) or hydronium ions (H3O+) when dissolved in water. All acids contain H+ as a cation, which is responsible for their acidic properties and ability to conduct electricity.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of bases based on OH- ion production
concepts.description: Bases are substances that produce hydroxide ions (OH-) when dissolved in water. Bases that dissolve in water are called alkalis. The OH- ions are responsible for the basic properties of these solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of acids with metals
concepts.description: Acids react with metals to produce hydrogen gas and a salt. The general equation is: Acid + Metal → Salt + Hydrogen gas. Hydrogen gas produced can be tested by its 'pop' sound with a burning candle.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of bases with metals
concepts.description: Some bases react with metals (like zinc) to produce hydrogen gas and a salt. Only amphoteric metals like zinc and aluminium show this reaction with bases, unlike their reaction with all acids.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of acids with metal carbonates and hydrogencarbonates
concepts.description: Metal carbonates and hydrogencarbonates react with acids to produce a salt, carbon dioxide gas, and water. The CO2 gas can be detected by turning lime water milky. General equation: Metal carbonate/hydrogencarbonate + Acid → Salt + CO2 + H2O.

concepts.concept_title: Neutralisation reaction between acids and bases
concepts.description: When an acid reacts with a base, they neutralise each other to form a salt and water. The ionic equation is: H+(aq) + OH-(aq) → H2O(l). This is used in everyday applications like treating antacids for indigestion.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of metallic oxides with acids
concepts.description: Metal oxides react with acids to produce a salt and water, similar to base-acid reactions. Because of this property, metal oxides are called basic oxides. General equation: Metal oxide + Acid → Salt + Water.

concepts.concept_title: pH scale and measurement of acidity/basicity
concepts.description: The pH scale (0-14) measures the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution. pH less than 7 indicates acidic, pH = 7 indicates neutral, and pH greater than 7 indicates basic solutions. Universal indicators show different colours at different pH values.

concepts.concept_title: Strong and weak acids and bases
concepts.description: Strong acids (like HCl, H2SO4) produce more H+ ions in solution, while weak acids (like acetic acid) produce fewer H+ ions at the same concentration. Similarly, strong bases produce more OH- ions and weak bases produce fewer OH- ions.

concepts.concept_title: Salts: classification as acidic, basic, or neutral
concepts.description: Salts formed from strong acid + strong base are neutral (pH=7). Salts from strong acid + weak base are acidic (pH<7). Salts from weak acid + strong base are basic (pH>7). The nature of salt depends on the relative strength of the acid and base used to form it.

concepts.concept_title: Chlor-alkali process and production of useful chemicals
concepts.description: When electricity is passed through aqueous sodium chloride (brine), it decomposes to form sodium hydroxide, chlorine gas, and hydrogen gas. This process is used industrially to produce these important chemicals.

concepts.concept_title: Bleaching powder: preparation and uses
concepts.description: Bleaching powder is produced by the action of chlorine gas on dry slaked lime [Ca(OH)2]. It is used for bleaching textiles, paper pulp, and clothes; as an oxidising agent; and for disinfecting drinking water.

concepts.concept_title: Baking soda and washing soda: preparation and uses
concepts.description: Baking soda (NaHCO3) is a mild base used in cooking, antacids, and fire extinguishers. Washing soda (Na2CO3.10H2O) is used for cleaning, removing water hardness, and in glass/soap manufacturing. Both are derived from sodium chloride.

concepts.concept_title: Water of crystallisation in salt hydrates
concepts.description: Water of crystallisation is a fixed number of water molecules present in one formula unit of a salt crystal. For example, CuSO4.5H2O contains 5 water molecules. When heated, these water molecules are removed, changing the colour and properties of the salt.

concepts.concept_title: Physical and Chemical Changes
concepts.description: Physical changes alter the appearance of matter without changing its chemical composition (e.g., melting ice), while chemical changes create new substances with different properties (e.g., burning magnesium). Chemical reactions involve breaking and forming bonds between atoms.

concepts.concept_title: Identifying Chemical Reactions
concepts.description: Chemical reactions can be identified by observable changes: change in state, change in colour, evolution of gas, or change in temperature. These observations help determine whether a chemical reaction has occurred.

concepts.concept_title: Word Equations and Chemical Equations
concepts.description: Chemical reactions can be represented as word equations (using substance names) or chemical equations (using chemical formulae). Reactants are written on the left side and products on the right side, separated by an arrow.

concepts.concept_title: Skeletal Chemical Equations
concepts.description: A skeletal chemical equation uses chemical formulae to represent reactants and products but may have unequal numbers of atoms on both sides. It serves as the starting point before balancing.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Conservation of Mass
concepts.description: Mass cannot be created or destroyed during a chemical reaction. The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products, meaning the number of atoms of each element must be the same before and after the reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing Chemical Equations
concepts.description: Chemical equations are balanced by adding coefficients (not changing subscripts in formulae) so that the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides. This is done using the hit-and-trial method with smallest whole numbers.

concepts.concept_title: Physical States in Chemical Equations
concepts.description: Physical states of reactants and products are indicated using symbols: (s) for solid, (l) for liquid, (g) for gas, and (aq) for aqueous solution. Reaction conditions like temperature, pressure, and catalyst can be written above/below the arrow.

concepts.concept_title: Combination Reactions
concepts.description: A combination reaction occurs when two or more substances (elements or compounds) combine to form a single product. Example: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂. Combination reactions are often exothermic, releasing heat.

concepts.concept_title: Decomposition Reactions
concepts.description: A decomposition reaction is the opposite of combination; a single substance breaks down into two or more simpler substances. Thermal decomposition occurs when heat is applied. Example: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. Decomposition reactions are endothermic.

concepts.concept_title: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions
concepts.description: Exothermic reactions release heat energy along with products, making the surroundings warm. Endothermic reactions absorb energy from surroundings. Respiration and combustion are exothermic; decomposition reactions are typically endothermic.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement Reactions
concepts.description: A displacement reaction occurs when a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound. Example: Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu. The element that displaces must be more chemically reactive.

concepts.concept_title: Double Displacement Reactions
concepts.description: A double displacement reaction involves the exchange of ions between two compounds. Example: Na₂SO₄ + BaCl₂ → BaSO₄ + 2NaCl. If an insoluble product forms, it's also called a precipitation reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Precipitation Reactions
concepts.description: A precipitation reaction is a type of double displacement reaction where an insoluble solid product (precipitate) forms when two solutions react. Example: BaCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄↓ + 2NaCl. The precipitate is the insoluble compound formed.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation and Reduction
concepts.description: Oxidation is the gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen by a substance. Reduction is the loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen. In oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions, one substance is oxidised while another is reduced simultaneously. Example: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O.

concepts.concept_title: Corrosion and Rancidity
concepts.description: Corrosion is the oxidation of metals when attacked by oxygen, moisture, or acids (e.g., rusting of iron, tarnishing of silver). Rancidity is the oxidation of fats and oils, causing changes in taste and smell. Both are oxidation reactions that can be prevented or slowed.

concepts.concept_title: Physical properties of metals (lustre, malleability, ductility)
concepts.description: Metals exhibit characteristic physical properties including metallic lustre (shiny surface), malleability (ability to be beaten into sheets), and ductility (ability to be drawn into wires). These properties vary among different metals but are generally distinctive of metallic elements.

concepts.concept_title: Thermal and electrical conductivity in metals
concepts.description: Metals are good conductors of both heat and electricity, with some metals like silver and copper being the best conductors. This property makes metals suitable for electrical wiring and heat transfer applications.

concepts.concept_title: Sonority of metals
concepts.description: Metals produce sound when struck against a hard surface, a property called sonority. This property is utilized in applications like school bells which are made from metal alloys.

concepts.concept_title: Exceptions to metal physical properties
concepts.description: Not all metals follow typical physical property patterns. For example, mercury is liquid at room temperature, gallium and caesium have low melting points, and alkali metals are soft enough to cut with a knife. These exceptions show that physical properties alone cannot classify elements.

concepts.concept_title: Formation of metal oxides through oxidation
concepts.description: When metals burn in air or oxygen, they combine with oxygen to form metal oxides. Most metal oxides are basic in nature, but some like aluminium oxide and zinc oxide are amphoteric, showing both acidic and basic properties.

concepts.concept_title: Reactivity series of metals
concepts.description: Metals exhibit different levels of reactivity with oxygen, water, and acids. A reactivity series can be determined experimentally by observing which metals react with water, acids, and whether they can displace other metals from salt solutions. Potassium is most reactive while gold is least reactive.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of metals with water and steam
concepts.description: Different metals react with water and steam to varying degrees. Highly reactive metals like sodium and potassium react violently with cold water, moderately reactive metals like magnesium react with hot water or steam, while less reactive metals like copper do not react with water at all.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of metals with dilute acids
concepts.description: Reactive metals displace hydrogen from dilute acids to form salt and hydrogen gas. The rate of hydrogen gas evolution and heat released varies with metal reactivity. Copper does not react with dilute hydrochloric acid, demonstrating its low reactivity.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement reactions between metals and metal salt solutions
concepts.description: A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from its salt solution. This displacement reaction can be used to determine the relative reactivity of metals and arrange them in a reactivity series.

concepts.concept_title: Ionic bonding and formation of ionic compounds
concepts.description: Metals lose electrons to form cations, while non-metals gain electrons to form anions. These oppositely charged ions attract each other through electrostatic forces, forming ionic compounds or electrovalent compounds like sodium chloride and magnesium chloride.

concepts.concept_title: Properties of ionic compounds
concepts.description: Ionic compounds are solids with high melting and boiling points due to strong electrostatic forces between ions. They are soluble in water but insoluble in non-polar solvents. They conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved in water when ions can move freely.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of metals by occurrence and extraction methods
concepts.description: Metals are found in the earth's crust as free elements (like gold and silver) or as compounds (like oxides, sulphides, carbonates). Highly reactive metals like sodium and calcium are found only as compounds. The extraction method depends on the metal's position in the reactivity series.

concepts.concept_title: Metal extraction from ores by reduction with carbon
concepts.description: Metal oxides can be reduced to metals by heating with carbon (coke). This method works for moderately reactive metals like zinc and iron whose oxides are reduced when heated with carbon to produce the metal and carbon monoxide or dioxide.

concepts.concept_title: Metal extraction by electrolytic reduction
concepts.description: Highly reactive metals at the top of the reactivity series (sodium, magnesium, calcium, aluminium) cannot be reduced with carbon. They are extracted by electrolysis of their molten salts, where the metal is deposited at the cathode and non-metal at the anode.

concepts.concept_title: Corrosion and prevention methods
concepts.description: Corrosion is the degradation of metals when exposed to moist air, such as rusting of iron. It requires both air and water to occur. Corrosion can be prevented by coating with paint, oil, zinc (galvanisation), or by making alloys like stainless steel.

concepts.concept_title: Covalent bonding and electron sharing
concepts.description: Covalent bonds form when two atoms share valence electrons to achieve a stable electron configuration. This sharing creates strong bonds within molecules but results in weak intermolecular forces, explaining the low melting and boiling points of most carbon compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Tetravalency of carbon
concepts.description: Carbon has four valence electrons and always forms four covalent bonds to achieve a stable octet configuration. This property allows carbon to bond with four other atoms, which can be other carbon atoms or atoms of other elements like hydrogen, oxygen, or nitrogen.

concepts.concept_title: Catenation in carbon compounds
concepts.description: Catenation is the unique ability of carbon to form strong bonds with other carbon atoms, creating long chains, branched chains, and rings. This property is responsible for the enormous variety of carbon compounds, as no other element exhibits catenation to such an extent.

concepts.concept_title: Saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Saturated hydrocarbons contain only single bonds between carbon atoms and are less reactive, while unsaturated hydrocarbons contain one or more double or triple bonds between carbon atoms and are more reactive. The presence of multiple bonds affects the chemical reactivity of these compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Structural isomerism
concepts.description: Structural isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements of atoms. This phenomenon occurs in carbon compounds due to the variety of ways carbon chains can be arranged as straight chains, branched chains, or rings.

concepts.concept_title: Functional groups and their properties
concepts.description: Functional groups are specific atoms or groups of atoms attached to carbon chains that determine the chemical properties of organic compounds. Examples include hydroxyl (-OH) in alcohols, aldehyde (-CHO), ketone (-CO-), and carboxyl (-COOH) in carboxylic acids, regardless of chain length.

concepts.concept_title: Homologous series
concepts.description: A homologous series is a group of compounds with the same functional group where consecutive members differ by a CH₂ unit. Members show gradation in physical properties with increasing molecular mass but have similar chemical properties determined by the functional group.

concepts.concept_title: Combustion of carbon and carbon compounds
concepts.description: Carbon and its compounds undergo combustion reactions with oxygen, releasing heat and light. Complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water, while incomplete combustion (limited oxygen) produces carbon (soot) along with carbon dioxide and water.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation of organic compounds
concepts.description: Alcohols can be oxidized to aldehydes, ketones, or carboxylic acids using oxidizing agents like alkaline potassium permanganate or acidified potassium dichromate. This selective oxidation converts -OH groups to C=O groups without breaking the carbon chain.

concepts.concept_title: Addition reactions in unsaturated hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Unsaturated hydrocarbons containing C=C or C≡C bonds can undergo addition reactions where hydrogen, halogens, or water are added across the multiple bonds in the presence of catalysts. This converts unsaturated compounds to saturated compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Substitution reactions in saturated hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Saturated hydrocarbons undergo substitution reactions where hydrogen atoms are replaced by other atoms or groups (like chlorine) in the presence of sunlight. One hydrogen atom is replaced at a time, producing multiple products with higher homologues.

concepts.concept_title: Esterification reaction
concepts.description: Esters are formed when a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst, with water as a byproduct. This reaction is reversible; treating esters with alkali (saponification) regenerates the alcohol and sodium salt of the carboxylic acid.

concepts.concept_title: Soap formation and cleaning mechanism
concepts.description: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long-chain carboxylic acids with both hydrophobic and hydrophilic ends. In water, soaps form micelles where hydrophobic tails surround oily dirt and hydrophilic ionic ends face outward, allowing dirt to be suspended and rinsed away.

concepts.concept_title: Hardness of water and role of detergents
concepts.description: Hard water contains calcium and magnesium ions that form insoluble precipitates (scum) with soaps, reducing their cleaning effectiveness. Detergents are sulphonic acid salts that don't form precipitates with these ions, making them effective cleaners in hard water.

concepts.concept_title: Allotropes of carbon and their structures
concepts.description: Carbon exists as different allotropes (diamond, graphite, fullerenes) with different atomic arrangements. Diamond has each carbon bonded to four others in a rigid 3D structure, while graphite has layers of hexagonal arrays where each carbon bonds to three others, explaining their different physical properties.

concepts.concept_title: Lewis Symbols and Valence Electrons
concepts.description: Lewis symbols represent valence electrons of atoms using dots around the element symbol. The number of dots indicates the number of valence electrons available for bonding, which helps determine the element's group valence and bonding behavior.

concepts.concept_title: Electrovalent (Ionic) Bond Formation
concepts.description: Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred from one atom to another, creating cations and anions held together by electrostatic attraction. This process is driven by atoms achieving noble gas configurations through electron loss or gain.

concepts.concept_title: Covalent Bond and Electron Sharing
concepts.description: Covalent bonds form when two atoms share electron pairs to achieve noble gas configurations. Single, double, and triple bonds result from sharing one, two, or three electron pairs respectively between atoms.

concepts.concept_title: Lewis Dot Structures of Molecules
concepts.description: Lewis structures represent molecules by showing valence electrons, bonding pairs, and lone pairs around atoms. These structures help visualize molecular composition and the distribution of electrons in bonding and non-bonding pairs.

concepts.concept_title: Octet Rule and Its Limitations
concepts.description: The octet rule states that atoms achieve stability by having eight electrons in their valence shell. However, it has limitations: incomplete octets (in BeH₂, BCl₃), odd-electron molecules (NO, NO₂), and expanded octets (in PF₅, SF₆) do not follow this rule.

concepts.concept_title: Lattice Enthalpy and Ionic Compound Stability
concepts.description: Lattice enthalpy is the energy required to completely separate one mole of an ionic solid into gaseous ions. It is the primary factor determining the stability of ionic compounds, as it compensates for the energy cost of ion formation.

concepts.concept_title: Bond Parameters: Length, Angle, and Enthalpy
concepts.description: Bond length is the distance between bonded nuclei, bond angle is the angle between bonding orbitals around a central atom, and bond enthalpy is the energy required to break one mole of bonds. These parameters characterize molecular structure and stability.

concepts.concept_title: Resonance Structures and Resonance Hybrid
concepts.description: When a single Lewis structure cannot accurately describe a molecule, multiple resonance structures with similar energy are written. The actual structure is a resonance hybrid that averages the characteristics of all canonical forms, explaining intermediate bond properties.

concepts.concept_title: Bond Polarity and Dipole Moment
concepts.description: Polar covalent bonds form when electrons are unequally shared between atoms of different electronegativities. Dipole moment measures this polarity as the product of charge magnitude and distance between charge centers, indicating molecular asymmetry.

concepts.concept_title: VSEPR Theory and Molecular Geometry Prediction
concepts.description: The Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory predicts molecular shapes based on the repulsion between electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) around the central atom. Lone pair-lone pair repulsion is greater than lone pair-bond pair, which is greater than bond pair-bond pair repulsion.

concepts.concept_title: Orbital Overlap and Covalent Bond Formation
concepts.description: According to valence bond theory, covalent bonds form through overlapping of atomic orbitals from different atoms. The greater the extent of orbital overlap, the stronger the resulting bond and the lower the potential energy of the system.

concepts.concept_title: Sigma and Pi Bonds
concepts.description: Sigma (σ) bonds form from end-to-end (head-on) overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis and can rotate freely. Pi (π) bonds form from sidewise overlap perpendicular to the internuclear axis and restrict rotation. Multiple bonds contain one σ bond and one or more π bonds.

concepts.concept_title: Hybridization of Atomic Orbitals (sp, sp², sp³)
concepts.description: Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new equivalent orbitals with different shapes and orientations. sp hybridization produces linear geometry, sp² produces trigonal planar, and sp³ produces tetrahedral geometry, explaining the shapes of molecules like BeCl₂, BCl₃, and CH₄.

concepts.concept_title: Hybridization Involving d Orbitals (sp³d, sp³d²)
concepts.description: Elements in the third period and beyond can use d orbitals in hybridization. sp³d hybridization (as in PCl₅) produces trigonal bipyramidal geometry, while sp³d² hybridization (as in SF₆) produces octahedral geometry, explaining expanded octets.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Orbital Theory and Bond Order
concepts.description: Molecular orbital theory describes bonding using molecular orbitals formed from linear combinations of atomic orbitals. Bonding molecular orbitals lower energy and increase electron density between nuclei, while antibonding orbitals raise energy. Bond order (half the difference between bonding and antibonding electrons) determines bond strength and stability.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrogen Bonding
concepts.description: Hydrogen bonding forms when a hydrogen atom bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N) is attracted to another electronegative atom. It can be intermolecular (between molecules) or intramolecular (within a molecule) and significantly affects physical properties and molecular structure.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of matter into pure substances and mixtures
concepts.description: Matter can be classified as pure substances (with fixed composition and uniform properties) or mixtures (containing two or more pure substances in variable ratios). Pure substances have characteristic properties while mixtures can be separated by physical methods.

concepts.concept_title: Elements vs compounds as types of pure substances
concepts.description: Elements contain only one type of atom or molecule, while compounds are formed when atoms of two or more different elements combine in a fixed ratio. Compounds have properties different from their constituent elements.

concepts.concept_title: Physical and chemical properties of substances
concepts.description: Physical properties (like color, melting point, density) can be measured without changing the substance's identity, while chemical properties (like combustibility, acidity) require a chemical change to be observed. Both types characterize substances.

concepts.concept_title: SI units and base quantities in chemistry
concepts.description: The SI system provides seven base units (meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) for measuring physical quantities. These standardized units allow consistent and comparable measurements across the scientific world.

concepts.concept_title: Conversion of units using dimensional analysis
concepts.description: Dimensional analysis (factor label method) allows conversion of measured quantities from one unit system to another by using unit factors that equal one. Units can be multiplied, divided, and canceled like algebraic quantities.

concepts.concept_title: Scientific notation for very large and very small numbers
concepts.description: Scientific notation expresses numbers as N × 10^x where N is between 1.000 and 9.999 and x is the exponent. This notation simplifies calculations with extremely large or small numbers common in chemistry.

concepts.concept_title: Significant figures and uncertainty in measurement
concepts.description: Significant figures represent meaningful digits in a measurement (certain digits plus one uncertain digit). The number of significant figures indicates the precision of the measurement and should be maintained through calculations.

concepts.concept_title: Mass, molar mass, and atomic mass units
concepts.description: Atomic mass (in amu or u) is the mass of an atom relative to carbon-12 standard. Molar mass is the mass in grams of one mole of substance, numerically equal to atomic/molecular mass. These concepts relate microscopic and macroscopic scales.

concepts.concept_title: Mole concept and Avogadro's number
concepts.description: The mole is the SI unit for amount of substance, defined as exactly 6.02214076 × 10^23 elementary entities. One mole of any substance contains the same number of particles, allowing conversion between atomic scale and macroscopic quantities.

concepts.concept_title: Laws of chemical combination (conservation of mass, definite proportions, multiple proportions)
concepts.description: The law of conservation of mass states matter is neither created nor destroyed in reactions. The law of definite proportions states compounds always contain the same elements in fixed mass ratios. The law of multiple proportions states different compounds of same elements have mass ratios as small whole numbers.

concepts.concept_title: Percentage composition and empirical formula determination
concepts.description: Mass percent of elements in a compound is calculated from molar mass and atomic composition. From mass percent data, the empirical formula (simplest whole number ratio) can be determined by converting to moles and finding the smallest ratio.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular formula from empirical formula and molar mass
concepts.description: The molecular formula is determined by finding the ratio of molar mass to empirical formula mass (n value), then multiplying all subscripts in the empirical formula by this n value. The molecular formula shows exact numbers of atoms in a molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing chemical equations using stoichiometric coefficients
concepts.description: Chemical equations are balanced by adjusting coefficients (not subscripts) to ensure equal numbers of each element's atoms on both sides. Coefficients represent molar ratios of reactants and products in the reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Stoichiometric calculations and limiting reagent concept
concepts.description: Stoichiometry uses balanced equations to calculate amounts of reactants needed or products formed. The limiting reagent is the reactant that runs out first, determining the maximum amount of product that can form regardless of other reactant amounts.

concepts.concept_title: Molarity, molality, and concentration expressions in solutions
concepts.description: Molarity (M) is moles of solute per liter of solution, while molality (m) is moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. Mass percent and mole fraction are other ways to express concentration. Molarity changes with temperature while molality does not.

concepts.concept_title: Discovery of electrons through cathode rays
concepts.description: Cathode rays are negatively charged particles (electrons) emitted from the cathode in a discharge tube. Their properties—travel in straight lines, deflection in electric/magnetic fields, and independence from electrode material—establish electrons as fundamental atomic constituents.

concepts.concept_title: Charge-to-mass ratio of electron (e/m ratio)
concepts.description: J.J. Thomson determined the electron's e/m ratio as 1.76 × 10¹¹ C/kg by balancing electric and magnetic field deflections. This quantitative measurement provided the first precise characterization of the electron particle.

concepts.concept_title: Millikan's oil drop experiment and electron charge
concepts.description: Millikan measured the charge on an electron as 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C by observing the motion of charged oil droplets in an electric field. Combined with the e/m ratio, this determined the electron mass as 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg.

concepts.concept_title: Discovery of protons and neutrons
concepts.description: Protons were identified as positive ions from hydrogen gas with specific charge-to-mass ratios. Neutrons, discovered by Chadwick through alpha-particle bombardment, are electrically neutral particles with mass slightly greater than protons.

concepts.concept_title: Rutherford's nuclear model and alpha-particle scattering
concepts.description: Rutherford's gold foil experiment showed that most alpha particles pass through undeflected while few are scattered, proving the nucleus is a tiny, dense, positively charged region at the atom's center surrounded by empty space.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic number and mass number
concepts.description: Atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons in the nucleus and defines the element. Mass number (A) is the total count of protons and neutrons. These determine an atom's identity and isotopic variety.

concepts.concept_title: Isotopes and isobars
concepts.description: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same Z) with different numbers of neutrons (different A), showing identical chemical properties. Isobars have the same mass number but different atomic numbers and belong to different elements.

concepts.concept_title: Dual nature of electromagnetic radiation (wave and particle)
concepts.description: Electromagnetic radiation exhibits both wave properties (diffraction, interference, wavelength, frequency) and particle properties (photons with discrete energy). This dual behavior is fundamental to understanding light-matter interactions.

concepts.concept_title: Planck's quantum theory and photon energy
concepts.description: Planck proposed that electromagnetic radiation is emitted in discrete quanta (photons). Energy of a photon is E = hν, where h is Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s), connecting energy directly to frequency.

concepts.concept_title: Photoelectric effect and Einstein's explanation
concepts.description: When light strikes a metal, electrons are ejected only if frequency exceeds a threshold. Einstein explained this using photon energy: kinetic energy of ejected electron = hν − hν₀, where hν₀ is the work function.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic spectra: emission and absorption line spectra
concepts.description: Atoms emit or absorb light only at discrete wavelengths, producing line spectra characteristic of each element. Line spectra provide direct evidence for quantized energy levels in atoms and are used for element identification.

concepts.concept_title: Bohr's model of hydrogen atom and quantized orbits
concepts.description: Bohr proposed electrons move in fixed circular orbits with quantized energies and angular momentum (L = nℏ). Energy levels are En = −2.18 × 10⁻¹⁸/n² J, successfully explaining hydrogen's line spectrum through energy transitions between orbits.

concepts.concept_title: De Broglie's wave-particle duality of matter
concepts.description: De Broglie proposed that matter particles possess wave properties with wavelength λ = h/p, where p is momentum. This explains electron diffraction and forms the foundation for quantum mechanics treating electrons as waves.

concepts.concept_title: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle
concepts.description: It is impossible to simultaneously determine both position (Δx) and momentum (Δp) of a particle with arbitrary precision: Δx·Δp ≥ h/4π. This rules out definite electron trajectories and necessitates a probabilistic model of atomic structure.

concepts.concept_title: Quantum numbers and atomic orbitals
concepts.description: Four quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s) describe an electron's state: n defines the shell and size, l determines the subshell shape, m_l specifies spatial orientation, and m_s indicates spin. Orbitals are regions of high electron probability, not fixed orbits.

concepts.concept_title: Pauli exclusion principle and electron filling
concepts.description: No two electrons in an atom can have identical sets of four quantum numbers. Each orbital holds maximum two electrons with opposite spins. This principle governs the aufbau filling order and predicts electronic configurations of all elements.

concepts.concept_title: Modern Periodic Law and Atomic Number
concepts.description: The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers, not atomic weights. Atomic number (number of protons) is a more fundamental property than atomic mass for organizing elements.

concepts.concept_title: Electronic Configuration and Periodic Classification
concepts.description: Elements are classified in the periodic table based on their electronic configurations. The periodic law is a consequence of periodic variation in electronic configurations, which determine physical and chemical properties.

concepts.concept_title: Block Classification of Elements
concepts.description: Elements are classified into s-, p-, d-, and f-blocks based on the type of orbital (s, p, d, or f) that receives the last electron during aufbau. Block type indicates the azimuthal quantum number of the valence subshell.

concepts.concept_title: Period and Group Periodicity
concepts.description: Period number corresponds to the highest principal quantum number (n) of valence electrons. Group elements have similar valence shell electronic configurations and exhibit similar chemical properties due to the same number of valence electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic Radius Trends
concepts.description: Atomic radius decreases across a period (left to right) due to increasing effective nuclear charge, and increases down a group due to addition of electron shells and increased shielding by core electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Ionic Radius and Isoelectronic Species
concepts.description: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms due to fewer electrons, while anions are larger due to increased electron repulsion. Among isoelectronic species, ions with greater positive charge have smaller radii.

concepts.concept_title: Ionization Enthalpy and Periodic Trends
concepts.description: Ionization enthalpy (energy required to remove an electron) increases across a period and decreases down a group. Trends are explained by effective nuclear charge and shielding effects.

concepts.concept_title: Electron Gain Enthalpy
concepts.description: Electron gain enthalpy measures the energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom. It becomes more negative across a period and less negative down a group, reflecting the ease of anion formation.

concepts.concept_title: Electronegativity and Periodic Trends
concepts.description: Electronegativity (ability to attract shared electrons) increases across a period and decreases down a group. It is related to atomic radius and directly correlates with non-metallic character.

concepts.concept_title: Metallic and Non-Metallic Character
concepts.description: Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period (left to right). Non-metallic character increases from left to right and decreases down a group, inversely related to electronegativity.

concepts.concept_title: Valence and Oxidation States
concepts.description: Valence (combining capacity) for representative elements equals the number of valence electrons or eight minus valence electrons. Oxidation state represents the charge acquired by an atom based on electronegative considerations.

concepts.concept_title: Transition Elements and d-block Properties
concepts.description: Transition metals (d-block) have partially filled d-orbitals and show variable oxidation states, colored ions, paramagnetism, and catalytic properties. They form a bridge between active s-block metals and p-block elements.

concepts.concept_title: Inner Transition Elements and f-block
concepts.description: Lanthanoids (4f series) and Actinoids (5f series) are f-block elements where f-orbitals are being filled. Properties within each series are similar; actinoids are radioactive with complex chemistry.

concepts.concept_title: Anomalous Properties of Second Period Elements
concepts.description: First members of groups (Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F) show different properties from subsequent members due to small size, high charge/radius ratio, and availability of only four valence orbitals for bonding.

concepts.concept_title: Diagonal Relationships in Periodic Table
concepts.description: Elements in diagonal positions (e.g., Li and Mg, Be and Al) show similar properties due to similar charge/radius ratios and electronegativity values, demonstrating trends across periods and down groups.

concepts.concept_title: System, surroundings, and types of thermodynamic systems
concepts.description: A thermodynamic system is the part of the universe being studied, separated from surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary. Systems are classified as open (exchange of matter and energy), closed (exchange of energy only), or isolated (no exchange) based on what crosses their boundaries.

concepts.concept_title: State functions and state variables
concepts.description: State functions (like pressure, volume, temperature, internal energy) depend only on the current state of a system, not on how that state was reached. Their changes are path-independent, making them fundamentally different from path-dependent quantities like heat and work.

concepts.concept_title: Internal energy as a state function
concepts.description: Internal energy (U) represents the total energy of a system from all sources (chemical, electrical, mechanical). It is a state function that changes when heat is absorbed/released or work is done on/by the system. Change in internal energy depends only on initial and final states.

concepts.concept_title: First law of thermodynamics
concepts.description: The first law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed; mathematically expressed as ∆U = q + w, where ∆U is change in internal energy, q is heat absorbed, and w is work done on the system. It quantifies energy conservation in thermodynamic processes.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure-volume work and reversible processes
concepts.description: Work done during gas expansion/compression is w = -p_ex∆V. In reversible processes, external pressure infinitesimally exceeds system pressure, making work w_rev = -∫p_in dV. Reversible processes proceed through equilibrium states; irreversible processes do not.

concepts.concept_title: Enthalpy and enthalpy change
concepts.description: Enthalpy (H = U + pV) is a state function particularly useful for constant-pressure processes. Enthalpy change ∆H = q_p at constant pressure represents heat absorbed by the system, and ∆H = ∆U + ∆ngRT for gas reactions.

concepts.concept_title: Hess's law of constant heat summation
concepts.description: Since enthalpy is a state function, the enthalpy change for a reaction is the same regardless of whether it occurs in one step or multiple steps. This allows calculation of unmeasurable enthalpies by combining known reactions algebraically.

concepts.concept_title: Standard enthalpy of formation
concepts.description: Standard enthalpy of formation (∆_f H°) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states (pure form at 1 bar). By convention, ∆_f H° = 0 for elements in their reference states.

concepts.concept_title: Entropy and spontaneity of processes
concepts.description: Entropy (S) measures disorder/randomness in a system. For spontaneous processes, total entropy change ∆S_total = ∆S_system + ∆S_surroundings > 0. Entropy is a state function where ∆S = q_rev/T for reversible processes, and increases with disorder.

concepts.concept_title: Gibbs energy and spontaneity criterion
concepts.description: Gibbs energy (G = H - TS) combines enthalpy and entropy effects. For constant temperature and pressure: ∆G = ∆H - T∆S. A process is spontaneous if ∆G < 0, non-spontaneous if ∆G > 0, and at equilibrium if ∆G = 0.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Gibbs energy and equilibrium constant
concepts.description: Standard Gibbs energy change relates to equilibrium constant by ∆_r G° = -2.303 RT log K. This allows prediction of reaction extent and equilibrium position; ∆_r G° < 0 means K > 1 (products favored), and ∆_r G° > 0 means K < 1 (reactants favored).

concepts.concept_title: Extensive and intensive properties
concepts.description: Extensive properties (mass, volume, enthalpy, internal energy) depend on the amount of substance present. Intensive properties (temperature, density, pressure) are independent of quantity. Molar properties are intensive values derived from extensive properties.

concepts.concept_title: Bond enthalpy and bond dissociation enthalpy
concepts.description: Bond enthalpy is the energy required to break one mole of a covalent bond in gaseous molecules. For polyatomic molecules, mean bond enthalpy is used. Reaction enthalpy can be estimated as: ∆_r H ≈ (bond enthalpies of reactants) - (bond enthalpies of products).

concepts.concept_title: Calorimetry and measurement of heat changes
concepts.description: Calorimetry measures heat changes in chemical reactions using a calorimeter. At constant volume (bomb calorimeter), ∆U is measured directly; at constant pressure, ∆H is measured. Heat absorbed is calculated as q = C∆T, where C is heat capacity.

concepts.concept_title: Dynamic Equilibrium in Physical Processes
concepts.description: Equilibrium is a dynamic state where opposing processes occur simultaneously at equal rates, with no net change in macroscopic properties. This applies to phase transitions like solid-liquid, liquid-vapor, and solid-vapor equilibria where the system is closed and at constant temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Dynamic Equilibrium in Chemical Reactions
concepts.description: Chemical equilibrium occurs when forward and reverse reaction rates become equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. The system is dynamic as reactions continue in both directions but produce no net change in composition.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Chemical Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant Expression
concepts.description: At equilibrium, the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations (each raised to stoichiometric coefficients) equals a constant Kc. This relationship is expressed as Kc = [products]/[reactants] and applies to all reversible reactions at a fixed temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Constant for Gaseous Reactions (Kp)
concepts.description: For gaseous equilibria, the equilibrium constant can be expressed in terms of partial pressures (Kp) instead of concentrations (Kc). The relationship Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn connects the two constants, where Δn is the change in moles of gas.

concepts.concept_title: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Equilibria
concepts.description: Homogeneous equilibria involve all reactants and products in the same phase, while heterogeneous equilibria involve multiple phases. In heterogeneous equilibria, pure solids and liquids are excluded from the equilibrium expression as their concentrations are constant.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction Quotient (Q) and Direction of Reaction
concepts.description: The reaction quotient Q has the same form as the equilibrium constant K but uses non-equilibrium concentrations. Comparing Q with K predicts reaction direction: if Q < K, reaction proceeds forward; if Q > K, reaction proceeds backward; if Q = K, system is at equilibrium.

concepts.concept_title: Le Chatelier's Principle and Effect of Concentration Changes
concepts.description: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed by changing concentration of reactants or products, the system shifts to counteract the change. Adding a substance shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a substance shifts equilibrium toward it, as predicted by Le Chatelier's principle.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Pressure and Volume Changes on Equilibrium
concepts.description: Changing pressure by altering volume affects equilibrium only when the number of moles of gaseous reactants differs from gaseous products. The equilibrium shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas when pressure increases, as predicted by Le Chatelier's principle.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature Change on Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant
concepts.description: Temperature is the only factor that changes the equilibrium constant value. For exothermic reactions, increasing temperature decreases K and shifts equilibrium backward; for endothermic reactions, increasing temperature increases K and shifts equilibrium forward.

concepts.concept_title: Ionic Equilibrium and Ionization of Weak Acids and Bases
concepts.description: Weak acids and bases establish equilibrium between ionized and unionized forms in aqueous solution. The ionization constant Ka (for acids) and Kb (for bases) quantify the extent of ionization and measure acid/base strength, with larger values indicating stronger acids/bases.

concepts.concept_title: pH Scale and Hydrogen Ion Concentration
concepts.description: pH is defined as -log[H+] and provides a logarithmic scale for expressing hydrogen ion concentration. For aqueous solutions at 25°C: pH < 7 indicates acidic, pH = 7 indicates neutral, and pH > 7 indicates basic solutions. The relationship pH + pOH = 14 holds at 25°C.

concepts.concept_title: Buffer Solutions and pH Control
concepts.description: Buffer solutions resist changes in pH upon dilution or addition of small amounts of acid or base. They consist of either a weak acid with its conjugate base salt or a weak base with its conjugate acid salt. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation relates pH to the pKa and concentration ratio of buffer components.

concepts.concept_title: Salt Hydrolysis and pH of Salt Solutions
concepts.description: Salts undergo hydrolysis in water where cations or anions from weak acids/bases react with water. Salts of weak acids and strong bases produce basic solutions; salts of strong acids and weak bases produce acidic solutions; salts of weak acid and weak base pH depends on relative pKa and pKb values.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility Product Constant (Ksp) and Precipitation Equilibria
concepts.description: For sparingly soluble salts, the solubility product constant Ksp equals the product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric coefficients at equilibrium. When the reaction quotient Q exceeds Ksp, precipitation occurs; when Q is less than Ksp, dissolution occurs.

concepts.concept_title: Common Ion Effect on Solubility
concepts.description: Adding a common ion (from another source) to a saturated solution of a sparingly soluble salt decreases its solubility. This occurs because the added ion shifts the dissolution equilibrium backward according to Le Chatelier's principle, reducing the concentration of the other ion needed to maintain Ksp.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of oxidation and reduction using electron transfer
concepts.description: Oxidation is the loss of electrons by any species, while reduction is the gain of electrons. These processes always occur simultaneously in redox reactions. A species that loses electrons is oxidized, and a species that gains electrons is reduced.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidizing and reducing agents
concepts.description: An oxidizing agent (oxidant) is a substance that accepts electrons from another substance, causing that substance to be oxidized. A reducing agent (reductant) is a substance that donates electrons to another substance, causing that substance to be reduced.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation numbers and oxidation states
concepts.description: Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a chemical compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by an atom in the compound. It follows a set of rules and helps track electron transfer in redox reactions involving covalent compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Rules for assigning oxidation numbers
concepts.description: Specific rules determine oxidation numbers: free elements have zero, monatomic ions equal the charge, oxygen is usually -2 (exceptions in peroxides and with fluorine), hydrogen is +1 (except -1 in metal hydrides), halogens are -1 as halides, and algebraic sum in compounds equals zero.

concepts.concept_title: Identifying redox reactions using oxidation number change
concepts.description: A redox reaction is identified by assigning oxidation numbers to all elements before and after the reaction. If any element shows a change in oxidation number, the reaction is a redox reaction. An increase in oxidation number indicates oxidation, while a decrease indicates reduction.

concepts.concept_title: Combination redox reactions
concepts.description: Combination reactions occur when two or more substances combine to form a single product. For it to be a redox reaction, at least one reactant must be in elemental form. Examples include combustion reactions and synthesis reactions with non-metals.

concepts.concept_title: Decomposition redox reactions
concepts.description: Decomposition reactions break down a compound into two or more simpler products. For it to be a redox reaction, at least one product must be in elemental form and there must be a change in oxidation numbers of the constituent elements.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement redox reactions
concepts.description: In displacement reactions, an ion or atom in a compound is replaced by another ion or atom of a different element. This includes metal displacement (one metal displaces another from its salt) and non-metal displacement (such as hydrogen or halogen displacement).

concepts.concept_title: Disproportionation reactions
concepts.description: Disproportionation is a special type of redox reaction where a single element in one oxidation state is simultaneously oxidized and reduced, forming products with higher and lower oxidation states. The element must be capable of existing in at least three different oxidation states.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing redox equations using oxidation number method
concepts.description: This method involves identifying elements that change oxidation state, calculating the change in oxidation numbers, making the total increase equal to the total decrease by using appropriate coefficients, and then balancing atoms using H+ or OH- ions and water molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing redox equations using half-reaction method
concepts.description: This method separates the redox reaction into oxidation and reduction half-reactions, balances each independently for atoms (except O and H), adds water and H+/OH- ions to balance oxygen and hydrogen, adds electrons to balance charge, equalizes electrons in both halves, and then combines them.

concepts.concept_title: Redox couples and electrode processes
concepts.description: A redox couple consists of the oxidized and reduced forms of a substance involved in an oxidation-reduction half-reaction, represented as Oxidized form/Reduced form. In electrochemical cells, redox reactions occur at separate electrodes through indirect electron transfer via an external circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Standard electrode potential and relative reactivity
concepts.description: Standard electrode potential (E°) is the potential of an electrode under standard conditions (1 M concentration, 1 atm pressure, 298 K), measured relative to the hydrogen electrode (0.00 V). More negative E° values indicate stronger reducing agents, while more positive values indicate stronger oxidizing agents.

concepts.concept_title: Electron transfer versus electron shift in redox reactions
concepts.description: In ionic compounds like NaCl, electrons are completely transferred (electron transfer). In covalent compounds like HCl, electrons are only partially shifted toward the more electronegative atom (electron shift). Both scenarios can be analyzed using oxidation numbers by assuming complete electron transfer for bookkeeping purposes.

concepts.concept_title: Competitive electron transfer and reaction feasibility
concepts.description: When multiple redox couples are present, the couple with the higher (more positive) standard electrode potential preferentially accepts electrons. The position of equilibrium in a redox reaction depends on the difference in standard electrode potentials of the two redox couples involved.

concepts.concept_title: Tetravalence of Carbon and sp³, sp², sp Hybridization
concepts.description: Carbon forms four covalent bonds through hybridization of s and p orbitals, producing sp³ (tetrahedral), sp² (trigonal planar), and sp (linear) hybrid orbitals. The type of hybridization determines the geometry and bond properties of organic molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Sigma and Pi Bonds in Organic Molecules
concepts.description: Sigma (σ) bonds result from head-on overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis, while pi (π) bonds form from lateral (side-by-side) overlap of p orbitals. Pi bonds are more reactive and restrict rotation around multiple bonds.

concepts.concept_title: Structural Representations of Organic Compounds
concepts.description: Organic compounds can be represented using complete structural formulas, condensed formulas, and bond-line formulas. Each representation shows different levels of detail about atomic connectivity and molecular structure.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Organic Compounds by Structure
concepts.description: Organic compounds are classified as acyclic (aliphatic), cyclic (alicyclic and aromatic), and heterocyclic. Aliphatic compounds contain open or branched chains, while aromatic compounds contain benzene rings with characteristic stability.

concepts.concept_title: Functional Groups and Homologous Series
concepts.description: A functional group is an atom or group of atoms responsible for characteristic chemical properties. Homologous series are compounds with the same functional group differing by CH₂ units, showing similar chemical behavior.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC Nomenclature of Alkanes and Branched Chains
concepts.description: IUPAC naming rules involve identifying the longest carbon chain, numbering to give substituents lowest numbers, and using prefixes and suffixes to indicate position and nature of branches. Systematic names correlate structure with nomenclature.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC Nomenclature of Compounds with Functional Groups
concepts.description: Compounds containing functional groups are named by identifying the principal functional group (highest priority), using appropriate suffixes, and numbering the chain to give the principal group the lowest number. Subordinate groups are named as prefixes.

concepts.concept_title: Structural Isomerism (Chain, Position, Functional Group, and Metamerism)
concepts.description: Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. Types include chain isomerism (different carbon skeletons), position isomerism (different functional group positions), functional group isomerism (different functional groups), and metamerism (different alkyl chains on either side of a functional group).

concepts.concept_title: Heterolytic Cleavage and Formation of Carbocations and Carbanions
concepts.description: Heterolytic cleavage of a covalent bond results in unequal distribution of electrons, producing carbocations (electron-deficient carbon with positive charge) or carbanions (electron-rich carbon with negative charge). Carbocations are sp² hybridized and trigonal planar.

concepts.concept_title: Homolytic Cleavage and Formation of Free Radicals
concepts.description: Homolytic cleavage of a covalent bond results in equal distribution of electrons, producing free radicals (neutral species with unpaired electrons). Free radical stability increases in the order: methyl < primary < secondary < tertiary.

concepts.concept_title: Nucleophiles and Electrophiles
concepts.description: Nucleophiles are electron-rich reagents that donate electron pairs to electrophilic centers. Electrophiles are electron-deficient reagents that accept electron pairs from nucleophilic centers. Polar organic reactions involve attack of nucleophiles on electrophilic sites.

concepts.concept_title: Inductive Effect and Electron-Withdrawing/Donating Groups
concepts.description: The inductive effect is the permanent polarization of σ bonds caused by the electronegativity of adjacent atoms or groups. Electron-withdrawing groups (halogens, NO₂, CN) withdraw electron density, while electron-donating groups (alkyl) donate electron density, with effects diminishing over distance.

concepts.concept_title: Resonance and Resonance Structures
concepts.description: Resonance describes molecules that cannot be adequately represented by a single Lewis structure. Resonance structures are hypothetical contributors that together describe the actual structure. The actual structure is more stable than any single resonance contributor by the resonance stabilization energy.

concepts.concept_title: Chromatography: Principles of Adsorption and Partition
concepts.description: Chromatography separates mixtures based on differential adsorption (on adsorbents like silica gel) or partition (between stationary and mobile phases). Column chromatography and thin-layer chromatography use adsorption, while paper chromatography uses partition. Rf values help identify separated components.

concepts.concept_title: Quantitative Analysis: Estimation of Carbon, Hydrogen, and Nitrogen
concepts.description: Carbon and hydrogen are estimated by burning the organic compound in oxygen and measuring CO₂ and H₂O produced. Nitrogen is estimated by Dumas method (volume of N₂ gas evolved) or Kjeldahl's method (conversion to ammonium salt and titration). Percentage composition is calculated from product masses.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of hydrocarbons by bond type
concepts.description: Hydrocarbons are classified into saturated (alkanes with only single bonds), unsaturated (alkenes and alkynes with double or triple bonds), and aromatic hydrocarbons based on the types of carbon-carbon bonds present.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC nomenclature of alkanes
concepts.description: Alkanes are named using IUPAC rules by identifying the longest carbon chain, numbering it to give lowest numbers to substituents, and naming alkyl groups as prefixes. Understanding primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary carbons is essential.

concepts.concept_title: Structural isomerism in alkanes
concepts.description: Alkanes with the same molecular formula can exist as chain isomers (different carbon skeleton arrangements) and positional isomers (different positions of substituents). The number of isomers increases significantly with molecular weight.

concepts.concept_title: Preparation of alkanes from alkyl halides and carboxylic acids
concepts.description: Alkanes can be prepared by reduction of alkyl halides using zinc and HCl, Wurtz reaction with sodium, or decarboxylation of carboxylic acid salts using soda lime or Kolbe's electrolytic method.

concepts.concept_title: Conformations of ethane and free rotation about C-C bonds
concepts.description: Alkanes exhibit infinite conformations due to free rotation about C-C single bonds. Staggered conformation is most stable with minimum torsional strain, while eclipsed conformation has maximum strain. The energy barrier between conformations is small (12.5 kJ/mol).

concepts.concept_title: Halogenation of alkanes via free radical mechanism
concepts.description: Alkanes undergo substitution with halogens through a chain mechanism involving initiation, propagation, and termination steps. The reactivity follows F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂, and tertiary hydrogens are replaced faster than secondary and primary hydrogens.

concepts.concept_title: Alkene nomenclature and structural isomerism
concepts.description: Alkenes are named using IUPAC rules with the suffix 'ene' and numbering from the end nearer the double bond. They show both chain and position isomerism, similar to alkanes.

concepts.concept_title: Geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism in alkenes
concepts.description: Alkenes with different groups on each carbon of the double bond exhibit cis-trans isomerism due to restricted rotation around the C=C bond. Cis isomers have identical groups on the same side and are more polar than trans isomers.

concepts.concept_title: Addition of hydrogen halides to unsymmetrical alkenes (Markovnikov's rule)
concepts.description: When HX adds to unsymmetrical alkenes, the hydrogen attaches to the carbon with more hydrogen atoms, and the halogen to the carbon with fewer hydrogens. This follows from carbocation stability and electrophilic addition mechanism.

concepts.concept_title: Anti-Markovnikov addition (peroxide effect) of HBr to alkenes
concepts.description: In the presence of peroxides, HBr adds to alkenes opposite to Markovnikov's rule, forming primary alkyl bromides as major products. This occurs via a free radical chain mechanism where secondary radicals are more stable than primary ones.

concepts.concept_title: Alkynes nomenclature and structure
concepts.description: Alkynes contain triple bonds (one σ and two π bonds) and are named with the suffix 'yne'. The C≡C bond is shorter and stronger than C=C and C-C bonds. Alkynes show acidic character due to sp hybridization, distinguishing them from alkenes.

concepts.concept_title: Benzene structure and aromaticity (resonance and Hückel rule)
concepts.description: Benzene is a resonance hybrid of Kekulé structures with six delocalized π electrons. It follows Hückel's rule (4n+2 π electrons) and exhibits aromatic character with planarity and equal C-C bond lengths (139 pm), intermediate between single and double bonds.

concepts.concept_title: Electrophilic substitution reactions of benzene
concepts.description: Benzene undergoes nitration, halogenation, sulphonation, and Friedel-Crafts alkylation/acylation via electrophilic substitution. These reactions proceed through formation of arenium ion intermediates and require Lewis acid catalysts, preserving the aromatic ring.

concepts.concept_title: Directive effects of substituents in monosubstituted benzene
concepts.description: Substituents on benzene direct incoming electrophiles to specific positions. Electron-donating groups (OH, NH₂, OCH₃) are ortho/para-directing and activating, while electron-withdrawing groups (NO₂, CN, CHO) are meta-directing and deactivating due to resonance and inductive effects.

concepts.concept_title: Carcinogenicity and toxicity of polynuclear hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons with fused benzene rings (like naphthalene derivatives) produced during incomplete combustion are toxic and carcinogenic. They enter the body, undergo biochemical reactions, damage DNA, and cause cancer.

concepts.concept_title: Matter defined by mass and volume
concepts.description: Matter is any substance that occupies space and has mass. Everything in our surroundings—air, water, stones, plants, animals—is made of matter possessing both these fundamental properties.

concepts.concept_title: Matter is made of particles
concepts.description: Matter consists of tiny discrete particles rather than being continuous. These particles can be observed through experiments like dissolving salt or sugar in water, where the substance spreads throughout the liquid.

concepts.concept_title: Particles of matter are extremely small
concepts.description: Matter particles are so small that a few crystals of potassium permanganate can colour several litres of water. This demonstrates that matter particles are beyond our everyday perception of size.

concepts.concept_title: Space exists between particles of matter
concepts.description: There are gaps between particles of matter. Other substances can dissolve and spread into these spaces, as seen when salt dissolves in water or when we make tea or coffee.

concepts.concept_title: Particles of matter are continuously moving
concepts.description: All particles possess kinetic energy and move constantly. The rate of movement increases with temperature, which explains why diffusion and mixing processes occur faster at higher temperatures.

concepts.concept_title: Diffusion of matter
concepts.description: Diffusion is the spontaneous intermixing of particles of two different types of matter without external force. The rate of diffusion increases with temperature and depends on the state of matter.

concepts.concept_title: Forces of attraction between particles
concepts.description: Particles of matter attract each other through intermolecular forces. The strength of these forces varies among different materials and determines properties like rigidity and compressibility.

concepts.concept_title: Characteristics of solids
concepts.description: Solids have definite shape, distinct boundaries, fixed volume, and are rigid and incompressible. Their particles are tightly packed with strong forces of attraction holding them in fixed positions.

concepts.concept_title: Characteristics of liquids
concepts.description: Liquids have a fixed volume but no fixed shape, taking the shape of their container. They are fluid and can flow, with particles moving more freely than in solids but still closely packed.

concepts.concept_title: Characteristics of gases
concepts.description: Gases have no fixed shape or volume, filling the container completely. They are highly compressible, diffuse rapidly, and exert pressure on container walls due to random high-speed particle motion.

concepts.concept_title: Melting point and fusion
concepts.description: Melting point is the temperature at which a solid converts to liquid at atmospheric pressure. During melting, absorbed heat energy (latent heat of fusion) overcomes forces of attraction without changing temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Boiling point and vaporization
concepts.description: Boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid converts to gas at atmospheric pressure. During boiling, particles from the bulk of the liquid gain enough energy to overcome intermolecular forces and become vapour.

concepts.concept_title: Sublimation and deposition
concepts.description: Sublimation is the direct conversion of solid to gas without passing through the liquid state, as seen with camphor or solid CO₂. Deposition is the reverse process of gas directly converting to solid.

concepts.concept_title: Evaporation as a surface phenomenon
concepts.description: Evaporation is the conversion of liquid to vapour at any temperature below boiling point, occurring only at the liquid surface. Particles with higher kinetic energy escape from the surface into the vapour state.

concepts.concept_title: Factors affecting evaporation rate
concepts.description: The rate of evaporation depends on surface area, temperature, humidity, and wind speed. Increased surface area, higher temperature, lower humidity, and stronger wind all increase evaporation rate.

concepts.concept_title: Pure substance vs Mixture
concepts.description: A pure substance consists of a single type of particle with uniform chemical nature throughout, while a mixture contains two or more pure substances physically combined. Pure substances have constant composition and properties, whereas mixtures have variable composition.

concepts.concept_title: Homogeneous mixtures (Solutions)
concepts.description: Homogeneous mixtures have uniform composition and properties throughout at the observable level. Solutions are homogeneous mixtures where solute dissolves completely in solvent, creating a single phase with particles smaller than 1 nm that cannot be seen or filtered out.

concepts.concept_title: Heterogeneous mixtures
concepts.description: Heterogeneous mixtures have non-uniform composition and contain physically distinct parts visible to the naked eye. Examples include suspensions and some colloids where different phases are clearly observable.

concepts.concept_title: Solvent and Solute in solutions
concepts.description: In a solution, the solvent is the component present in larger amount that dissolves other components, while solute is the component dissolved in smaller amount. The solvent is typically the medium in which dissolution occurs, such as water in most common solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Concentration of a solution
concepts.description: Concentration is the amount of solute present per unit mass or volume of solution. It can be expressed as mass by mass percentage, mass by volume percentage, or volume by volume percentage, and determines whether a solution is dilute, concentrated, or saturated.

concepts.concept_title: Saturated and Unsaturated solutions
concepts.description: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a given temperature; adding more solute will not increase dissolution. An unsaturated solution contains less solute than the saturation level and can still dissolve more solute.

concepts.concept_title: Suspension
concepts.description: A suspension is a heterogeneous mixture where solid particles are dispersed in a liquid medium but do not dissolve. Suspension particles are visible to naked eye, scatter light, and settle out when left undisturbed, allowing separation by filtration.

concepts.concept_title: Colloidal solution
concepts.description: A colloid is a heterogeneous mixture with particle size between solutions and suspensions (1-1000 nm), appearing homogeneous to naked eye but actually heterogeneous. Colloidal particles scatter light (Tyndall effect), do not settle, and cannot be separated by filtration but can be separated by centrifugation.

concepts.concept_title: Tyndall effect
concepts.description: The Tyndall effect is the scattering of light by colloidal particles, making the path of light visible through the mixture. This effect helps distinguish colloids from true solutions, as solutions do not scatter light while colloids do.

concepts.concept_title: Physical change
concepts.description: A physical change is a change in the physical properties of matter (like state, shape, size, or color) without altering its chemical composition or creating new substances. Examples include melting, boiling, dissolving, and changes of state.

concepts.concept_title: Chemical change
concepts.description: A chemical change involves the rearrangement of atoms to form new substances with different chemical properties and composition. During chemical changes, bonds are broken and reformed, creating new materials that cannot be easily reversed to original substances.

concepts.concept_title: Element
concepts.description: An element is a pure substance composed of only one type of atom that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical reactions. Elements are the basic building blocks of all matter and are classified as metals, non-metals, or metalloids based on their properties.

concepts.concept_title: Compound
concepts.description: A compound is a pure substance formed when two or more different elements chemically combine in a fixed proportion. Compounds have properties different from their constituent elements and can only be decomposed into elements by chemical means, not physical separation.

concepts.concept_title: Differences between mixtures and compounds
concepts.description: Mixtures are formed by physical combination of substances in variable proportions and retain properties of constituents, while compounds are formed by chemical combination in fixed proportions with entirely new properties. Mixture components can be separated physically, but compound components require chemical methods for separation.

concepts.concept_title: Separation techniques for mixtures
concepts.description: Mixtures can be separated into pure substances using physical methods appropriate to their properties: filtration for suspensions, evaporation for solutions, magnetic separation for magnetic materials, and distillation or chromatography for complex mixtures. The choice depends on the nature and properties of mixture components.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Conservation of Mass
concepts.description: Mass cannot be created or destroyed during a chemical reaction. The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products in any chemical change.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Constant Proportions (Definite Proportions)
concepts.description: In any pure chemical compound, the elements are always present in the same fixed ratio by mass, regardless of the source or method of preparation. For example, water always contains hydrogen and oxygen in a 1:8 mass ratio.

concepts.concept_title: Dalton's Atomic Theory Postulates
concepts.description: Matter is composed of indivisible particles called atoms. Atoms of the same element are identical, atoms of different elements are different, and atoms combine in simple whole number ratios to form compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic Mass Unit and Relative Atomic Mass
concepts.description: One atomic mass unit (u) is defined as 1/12th the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Relative atomic mass of an element is the average mass of its atom compared to 1/12th of a carbon-12 atom.

concepts.concept_title: Element Symbols and Nomenclature
concepts.description: Elements are represented by one or two letter symbols, where the first letter is uppercase and the second is lowercase. Symbols often derive from element names in English, Latin, German, or Greek.

concepts.concept_title: Molecule Definition and Structure
concepts.description: A molecule is a group of two or more atoms chemically bonded together, representing the smallest particle of an element or compound capable of independent existence while retaining all properties of that substance.

concepts.concept_title: Atomicity of Molecules
concepts.description: Atomicity is the number of atoms present in a molecule of an element. Molecules can be monoatomic (1 atom), diatomic (2 atoms), triatomic (3 atoms), or polyatomic (many atoms).

concepts.concept_title: Ions and Ionic Charges
concepts.description: Ions are charged species formed when atoms gain or lose electrons. Cations are positively charged, anions are negatively charged. Polyatomic ions are groups of atoms carrying a net charge.

concepts.concept_title: Valency and Combining Capacity
concepts.description: Valency is the combining power of an element, representing the number of bonds an atom can form. It determines how atoms of one element combine with atoms of another to form compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Writing Chemical Formulae Using Valency
concepts.description: Chemical formulae are written by crossing over the valencies of combining elements or ions. The formula represents the composition and shows the number of atoms of each element in a compound.

concepts.concept_title: Binary Compounds and Their Formulae
concepts.description: Binary compounds contain exactly two different elements. Their formulae are determined by crossing the valencies of the two elements and simplifying to the lowest whole number ratio.

concepts.concept_title: Polyatomic Ions in Chemical Formulae
concepts.description: When a compound contains polyatomic ions (groups of atoms with a net charge), the ion formula is enclosed in brackets with a subscript indicating the number of ions present, such as Ca(OH)2.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Mass Calculation
concepts.description: Molecular mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule. It is calculated by multiplying the atomic mass of each element by the number of its atoms and adding all values.

concepts.concept_title: Formula Unit Mass
concepts.description: Formula unit mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a formula unit of an ionic compound. It is calculated similarly to molecular mass but applies to substances composed of ions rather than molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Determining Empirical Formula from Mass Ratios
concepts.description: The empirical formula of a compound can be determined from the mass ratio of its constituent elements by converting masses to mole ratios and simplifying to the smallest whole number ratio.
